low_sim.speaker: Socrates
low_sim.ref: What was your conversation? But it is obvious that Lysias entertained you with his speeche
low_sim.new: And how did he entertain you? Can I be wrong in supposing that Lysias gave you a feast of

low_sim.speaker: Socrates
low_sim.new: And will you go on with the narration?

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: Now listen to me. Stop trying to fool me; for I can say something which will force you to
low_sim.new: You see how matters stand; and therefore let there be no more pretences; for, indeed, I kn

low_sim.speaker: Socrates
low_sim.ref: Oh! Oh! You wretch! How well you found out how to make a lover of discourse do your will!
low_sim.new: Villain! I am conquered; the poor lover of discourse has no more to say.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: Only speak, and in other matters suit yourself.
low_sim.new: Only go on and you may do anything else which you please.

low_sim.speaker: Socrates
low_sim.ref: You don’t understand that the name of the approver is written first in the writings of sta
low_sim.new: Why, do you not know that when a politician writes, he begins with the names of his approv

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: And will she be right in saying this?
low_sim.new: There is reason in the lady's defence of herself.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: So be it; only make your meaning clear.
low_sim.new: Granted; if you will only please to get on.

low_sim.speaker: Socrates
low_sim.ref: Stop. Now we must tell what there is in this that is faulty and lacks art, must we not?
low_sim.new: Enough:--Now, shall I point out the rhetorical error of those words?

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: Certainly that of which he speaks is an ending.
low_sim.new: Yes, indeed, Socrates; he does begin at the end.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: Yes, and I found it very pleasant to hear.
low_sim.new: I know that I had great pleasure in listening to you.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: There are only little things, not worth mentioning.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: What do you mean by that?

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: That would, I think, be excellent.
low_sim.new: You have hit upon a very good way.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: No other way is possible, Socrates. But it seems a great task to attain to it.
low_sim.new: He must take this, Socrates, for there is no possibility of another, and yet the creation

low_sim.speaker: Socrates
low_sim.ref: But it is noble to strive after noble objects, no matter what happens to us.
low_sim.new: But even to fail in an honourable object is honourable.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: You are quite right about that, too.

low_sim.speaker: Phaedrus
low_sim.ref: By all means that is what I wish and pray for.
low_sim.new: That is most assuredly my desire and prayer.
